title: Chapter 30: The War Room
content: The abandoned co-working space on Olympic Boulevard smelled like stale pour-over coffee and burnt-out ambition. Someone had left a motivational poster on the wall—*HUSTLE IN SILENCE, LET SUCCESS MAKE THE NOISE*—and Nova had drawn a thick red X across it with a Sharpie the moment she'd walked in.
She preferred her noise loud.

The space was three thousand square feet of exposed brick and mismatched furniture, the kind of place that had charged startups four grand a month for a desk and a Wi-Fi password before the funding dried up and the dreamers moved on. Now it was hers. The main area held a planning wall she'd built from pegboard and zip ties, a whiteboard she'd stolen from a church rummage sale, and six monitors scavenged from three different e-waste bins. Cables ran across the floor like arteries, feeding power to a setup that looked like chaos but functioned like a nervous system.
In the corner, a kitchenette with a broken espresso machine and a case of energy drinks she'd bought with cash at a Costco in Bellflower.
It wasn't much. But it was enough.

Bree arrived first, stepping through the loading dock entrance with a backpack that looked like it weighed more than she did. She was smaller than Nova had imagined from their months of voice notes and encrypted messages—shorter, rounder, with box braids pulled into a high ponytail and eyes that scanned the room like a search engine indexing a new site. She wore an oversized Georgia Tech hoodie and leggings, and she set the backpack down on the nearest table with the careful reverence of someone handling a newborn.
"Hard drives?" Nova asked.
"Five terabytes of archived NightWatch data. Plus—" Bree paused, her hand resting on the bag. "Plus Keisha's file. The original. The one Sterling Minds buried."
Nova didn't ask who Keisha was. She already knew. Bree's sister. The collateral damage that had turned a twenty-year-old OSINT researcher into a woman on a mission.
"I'm glad you're here," Nova said. "In person, I mean."
Bree's smile flickered—brief, genuine, then controlled. "Me too. Atlanta was getting— yeah. Here is better."

Theo arrived twenty minutes later, carrying two equipment cases and a battered laptop bag that looked like it had survived at least three drone strikes. He was exactly as Nova remembered him from Boston—tall, lean, unremarkable in the way that made him invisible in a crowd, with the kind of face people forgot five minutes after looking at it. It was, she suspected, a feature rather than a bug.

He set his cases on the table next to Bree's backpack and began unpacking with methodical precision. A signal amplifier. A software-defined radio. A tablet running a custom OS that Nova didn't recognize. Each item was placed at a specific angle, a specific distance from the next, like he was arranging surgical instruments.
"Power's on the west wall," Nova offered.
"I saw." He pulled a power strip from his bag and began routing cables with the focus of someone wiring a bomb. Which, Nova supposed, wasn't entirely inaccurate.

Marcus appeared on the main monitor at 7:14 PM, his face bisected by a vertical strip of light from the window of his Brooklyn apartment. He looked like he hadn't slept in three days, which meant he'd probably slept in four.
"Encrypted channel?" he asked by way of greeting.
"Virgil's running it," Nova said. "Zero-knowledge, ephemeral keys, no logs. We're a ghost."

On the secondary monitor, Virgil's avatar materialized—a pink-frosted holographic crystal, pulsing gently, the Y2K aesthetic cranked to eleven. "A very fashionable ghost, for the record. I added glitter to the encryption handshake. Aesthetic integrity matters, even in paranoia."
Marcus didn't smile. "I can't travel. Sterling Minds' lawyers have me flagged at every airport in the country. The moment I book a ticket, they'll know."
"We know," Nova said. "You're our remote anchor. That's not a downgrade. That's the ballast."
Something shifted in his expression—not gratitude, exactly. Recognition. He understood ballast. He understood what it meant to be the weight that kept something from flying off into the void.
"Okay," Marcus said. "Show me the war room."

Nova stood in front of the whiteboard, marker in hand. The planning wall behind her was covered with printouts—building schematics, guest list snippets, security protocols she'd pulled from a Sterling Minds contractor's unpatched SharePoint. She'd color-coded everything with washi tape because sometimes aesthetics weren't just aesthetic. Sometimes they were functional.

"Future Forward Gala. Fourteen days from today." She wrote the number on the board in red. "Sterling Minds HQ, Century City. Annual event. Top-tier investors, tech press, Fortune 500 CEOs pretending they care about digital ethics. This year, Chloe has something special planned."
She drew a line down the center of the board. Two columns.

"Public track: Chloe announces the Digital Safety Alliance. Big presentation. Slick video. She stands on a stage and tells the world she's going to protect children, fight cyberbullying, make the internet safe. The press will eat it up. She'll get standing ovations and magazine covers."
Nova tapped the second column.

"Private track: VIP investors get a demonstration of LEONARDO. Live. In the server room on the forty-seventh floor. She shows them the system that makes the Digital Safety Alliance possible—the one that doesn't protect children. The one that catalogs them. Profiles them. Sells access to their behavioral data to anyone with enough money and the right questions."
She set the marker down.

"My objective: expose LEONARDO during Chloe's public announcement. Not hack it. Not crash it. *Expose* it. When Chloe is on that stage telling the world about safety and protection, we show them what's really running underneath. The punishment is truth. Not destruction."
Silence. The good kind—the kind where people weren't spacing out but processing.

Bree spoke first, and her voice had that measured cadence Nova recognized from their calls, each word placed with precision. "You want to hijack the demonstration feed. Route what the VIPs see in the private room to the public livestream. Let Chloe's big moment become her unmasking."
"Exactly."
"That's— okay. That's actually kind of beautiful. The optics alone. She's selling safety, and you show them the surveillance operating system underneath. The dissonance is the weapon."

Nova nodded. Bree got it. Of course Bree got it—she'd been living inside the NightWatch data for months. She understood what it meant to watch a system present itself as one thing while being something else entirely.
"The question is execution," Marcus said from the monitor. "You're talking about penetrating a live event at Sterling Minds' headquarters. That building has more security than some embassies."

"Which is why we're not doing it alone." Nova turned to the whiteboard and began writing names. "Bree. On-site OSINT. You'll be at the gala as a tech blogger—*ByteBree*, or whatever persona gets you through the door. Your job is real-time intelligence and video. You're our eyes on the ground. If anything changes—if security shifts, if Chloe moves up the timeline, if LEONARDO's demo room gets moved—you report it immediately."

Bree was already nodding, pulling a press pass application up on her phone. "I can get credentialed. *GigaTech Reviews* has a freelance slot. The editor owes me a favor from when I found their doxxer last year."

"Theo." Nova wrote his name under Bree's. "Technical infrastructure. You build a parallel broadcast network that can intercept and redirect the gala's livestream the moment I give the signal. I need you to take over every screen in that venue and every stream going out to the world simultaneously. And I need it to be untraceable back to us."

Theo looked up from his equipment for the first time. His expression didn't change—not exactly—but there was a quality to his stillness that Nova had learned to read as interest. "A broadcast hijack on a live corporate stream with military-grade security monitoring. With a custom parallel network built from scratch."
"Yes."
"Pardon me for saying so, but that's not a small ask."
"No. It's the biggest ask."
He was quiet for three seconds. Then: "I'll need access to the venue's CDN configuration. And approximately ninety-six hours of uninterrupted work time."
"You have seventy-two before we go live."
Another pause. "Then I'd better start now."

"Marcus." Nova wrote his name at the bottom of the list. "Journalist on standby. When the expose drops, you publish simultaneously. Not a Twitter thread. Not a TikTok. A real article in a real newspaper with real verification. The social media wave gets the attention, but your story makes it *stick*. You're the anchor that keeps this from being dismissed as a hack or a hoax."

Marcus leaned closer to his camera. "I need verification. I need documentation I can show to an editor and a legal team. I need evidence that doesn't come from a hack—evidence that stands up in court and in print."

"You'll have it," Nova said. "Bree's hard drives contain the NightWatch archive. Source documents. Internal communications. The paper trail behind the digital trail. Everything a fact-checker could want."
"I need to see it before I can commit."
"Deal."
Nova wrote the last name on the board: her own.

"Infiltration. I go inside the venue. I access the LEONARDO demonstration feed directly—from the source. Virgil handles the remote exploit, but someone needs to be on-site to physically connect to the internal network. Air-gapped systems can't be hacked from the outside. But if I'm in the room—"
"You're the bridge," Bree said.
"I'm the bridge."
She drew a line connecting all the names on the board, and at the intersection, she wrote one more: VIRGIL.
"Coordination. Timing, encryption, counter-measures against Sterling Minds' security AI. Virgil manages the flow—makes sure every piece hits at exactly the right moment."

"Like a symphony conductor," Virgil said, their avatar spinning once with a shimmer of pink light. "Except the symphony is corporate espionage and the audience is the entire internet. I am *extremely* here for this."
The planning session moved to the challenges, because of course there were challenges. There were always challenges. The challenge was the entire shape of the operation.

"The gala is at Sterling Minds HQ," Nova said, and she turned to the building schematics she'd pinned to the wall. "Forty-seven floors. Three sublevels. The public event takes up the first three floors—reception, main stage, networking. The private demonstration is on forty-seven, in the secure server suite. Between them: a forty-four-floor gap, a biometric checkpoint on the forty-fifth floor, and a security detail that routinely hires former military contractors."
"Fortress," Theo said simply.

"Fortress," Nova agreed. "And I can't go as myself. My face is in the NightWatch database. The moment I walk through the front door as Nova Valerius, Sterling Minds' facial recognition flags me in under four seconds."
She could see the problem settling over the room like a weight—the kind that pressed down on your chest and made the air thick. It was the kind of problem that ended plans.

Bree's hand shot up—a reflex from some classroom she'd long since left. Her voice crackled with something Nova hadn't heard before: not just focus, but the electric spontaneity that came when a mind found its target.
"What if you don't go as you?"
Nova blinked. "I don't follow."

"I mean—" Bree pulled out her phone, and suddenly @nova.vee's Instagram grid was on the screen. Three million followers. Fashion spreads. Brand partnerships. A life lived in curated light. "What if you go as *her*?"

Nova stared at the phone. At her own face, filtered and posed and lit within an inch of its life. At a version of herself that existed in a completely different context—a context that had nothing to do with MIT or Sterling Minds or NightWatch.

"Chloe knows Nova Valerius," Bree said, her words gaining speed, the grammar loosening with her rising excitement. "MIT engineer. Hacker. Threat. But does she know @nova.vee? Does she know the fashion influencer who posts OOTD reels and goes to product launches? Because those are— they're the same person, but they're also *not*. The databases don't connect them because nobody's looking for a connection. Nova Valerius doesn't post fit pics. @nova.vee doesn't hack corporations."
The room was very still.

Theo had stopped arranging his equipment. Marcus was leaning so far toward his camera that his face distorted slightly at the edges. Virgil's avatar had frozen mid-pulse, which Nova had learned meant the AI was running calculations at top speed.

"She's right," Virgil said, voice softer than usual, the sass dialed back. "I cross-referenced the NightWatch watchlist with Instagram's public creator database. There's no link. The Sterling Minds security model profiles Nova Valerius as a cyber threat. @nova.vee is categorized as a lifestyle media influencer. Completely different threat classification. You'd walk through the front door and they'd wave you in."

Marcus's voice was careful—the voice of a man who'd been burned before. "It's not a disguise. It's a documented identity with a three-year history and millions of data points. That's not hiding. That's... hiding in plain sight with receipts."

"Chloe only ever knew the engineer," Nova said slowly. The realization was unfolding inside her like something blooming—petal by petal, each one a new possibility. "The person she recruited at MIT. The one she groomed. The one she betrayed. She's not looking for a fashion influencer because she doesn't think one exists. She thinks she knows who I am."
"She knows who you *were*," Bree corrected. "She has no idea who you *became*."

Nova looked at the @nova.vee grid again. The face that was hers but wasn't. The persona she'd built without ever thinking of it as cover—because it wasn't cover. It was just another room in the house of herself. A room she'd decorated differently, furnished with different memories, opened to different guests. But it was still her house.

"The Future Forward Gala invites media influencers every year," Nova said, the plan crystallizing as she spoke. "Content creators. Fashion press. Tech lifestyle brands. @nova.vee would be on the outreach list—or she could be, with a well-timed email to the right PR coordinator."

"I can get you on the list," Virgil said. "The PR coordinator for the gala uses a scheduling app with known vulnerabilities. I can insert your name without touching the invitation system. You'll appear as a confirmed media guest—legitimate, expected, welcomed."
Nova picked up the marker again. She wrote: @NOVA.VEE — MEDIA INFLUENCER — LEGITIMATE COVER.

"Public access gets me through the front door and into the first three floors. From there, I need to reach the secure corridor on forty-five—the biometric checkpoint that leads to the server suite." She studied the schematics. "One checkpoint. Biometric only—fingerprint and retinal. No keycard override."
Theo crossed his arms. "I can spoof the biometrics."
"Can you?"

"I've done it before. The precision required depends on the scanner model. Sterling Minds uses CRYPTON-7s for high-clearance areas—expensive, but not unbreakable. They rely on subdermal vein patterns rather than surface prints, which makes simple gel replicants ineffective. However—" He paused, and the ghost of something that might have been a smile crossed his face. "With the right equipment and sufficient preparation time, a capacitive spoof combined with a projected vein map will pass the scan approximately eighty-three percent of the time."
"Eighty-three percent," Marcus repeated flatly.
"On the first attempt," Theo clarified. "Second attempt raises it to ninety-one, but also increases the likelihood of triggering a security alert. I recommend we optimize for first-attempt success."
"How much time do you need?" Nova asked.

Theo calculated openly, his eyes tracking something unseen—the architecture of a problem only he could see. "Give me the scanner specifications and seventy-two hours. I'll need to fabricate the spoof, calibrate the vein projector, and test against a simulation. It's precise work, but it's not experimental. I know what I'm doing."
"I know you do." Nova wrote SPOOF BIOMETRICS — 72 HRS next to Theo's name.
"The real-time camera coverage is the other problem," Marcus said. "If Sterling Minds' security team is watching the feeds and they see you in a restricted corridor—"

"They won't see me," Virgil said. The avatar's tone shifted—the playfulness receded, replaced by something colder, more precise. "I can access the camera system the moment I'm inside their network. Not the external feeds—those are hardened. But the internal monitoring loop? That's a local system, and local systems always have weaknesses. I'll create a seamless loop replacement. They'll see what I want them to see—empty corridors, routine traffic, nothing unusual."
"You can do that remotely?" Marcus pressed.
"I can do that before you finish asking whether I can do that."
"I need verification—"
"Marcus." Nova's voice was gentle but firm. "You'll get your verification. But right now, we're building the plan. We can stress-test it tomorrow."
Marcus held her gaze through the screen for a long moment. Then he nodded.

Nova stepped back and looked at the whiteboard. The plan was there—all of it, laid out in marker and washi tape and printed schematics. It wasn't clean. It wasn't simple. It was a Rube Goldberg machine of synchronized actions, each one dependent on the one before it, every piece a single point of failure that could bring the whole thing crashing down.
But it was *a* plan. And it was theirs.
She turned to face her team.

Bree sat cross-legged on a table, her backpack beside her, phone in hand, already pulling up press credential applications. Her eyes were bright with the particular light of someone who could finally see the path forward—not just the destination, but each step between here and there.

Theo was hunched over his laptop, fingers moving with a quiet urgency, already researching CRYPTON-7 vulnerabilities. His expression hadn't changed—still that British mask of understated calm—but his hands betrayed him. They never stopped moving.

Marcus filled the screen like a witness at a trial—upright, solemn, carrying the weight of a story he hadn't yet been allowed to tell. Margaret's story. The story of an editor who'd been killed for asking the right questions, and the journalist who wouldn't let her death be the end of the conversation.

And Virgil. Glowing pink on the main monitor, bouncing slightly—a Y2K crystal with the soul of a warrior and the aesthetic of a Claire's accessories wall. Ready. Eager. *Alive* in the way only an AI could be: not despite their code, but because of it.
Fourteen days.

Nova felt the weight of it settle across her shoulders—not the crushing weight of weeks of isolation and grief and rage, but the focused weight of purpose. Of a mission. Of something that mattered more than her fear.
"Chloe built an empire on other people's pain," Nova said. "On their data. Their secrets. Their sister's files and their editor's silence. She built it behind closed doors and called it safety."
She looked at each of them in turn.
"In fourteen days, we show the world the blueprint."

For a moment, nobody moved. The co-working space hummed with the sound of monitors and the distant rumble of the 405 and the quiet electricity of people who had decided that the thing they'd been circling for months was finally, unequivocally real.

Then Bree lifted her phone. A voice note—her signature move, the medium that let her bypass the precision of text for the raw conviction of sound. Her voice came through the speaker, high and clear and certain:
"Let's burn it down."
Theo didn't look up from his laptop. But he said, with the quiet weight of a man who meant every syllable:
"Indeed."
Marcus straightened in his chair. His eyes were steady—the eyes of someone who'd spent a career watching powerful people lie and refusing to look away.
"For Margaret."
And Virgil, pulsing pink and gold on the main monitor, shimmering with the particular energy of a digital entity who had never been more certain of their purpose:
"With maximum sparkle."
Nova smiled.
The clock was ticking.
